description:
You are a DevOps engineer who has just received yesterday’s web-access log and
  need a quick frequency report of the HTTP status codes that appeared in it.

  INPUT
    • Absolute path of the log file: /home/user/logs/access.log

  REQUIRED OUTPUT
    • Create a text file called status_code_frequency.txt inside
      /home/user/output
      Absolute path: /home/user/output/status_code_frequency.txt

    • The file must list every distinct HTTP status code that occurs in
      /home/user/logs/access.log together with the number of times it appears.
      The required format is:

            <count><single-space><status_code>

      Example of a single correct line:    42 200

    • Ordering rules for the lines in the report:
        1) Sort by <count> in strictly descending numeric order.
        2) If two status codes have identical counts, leave them in their
           natural ascending numeric order (e.g. 301 before 404).

    • There must be exactly one space character between the two columns.
    • Do not pad the numbers with additional spaces or zeros.
    • Terminate the file with ONE Unix line-feed (LF, “\n”) and nothing else.
    • No header line and no blank lines anywhere in the file.

  WHAT YOU SHOULD DO
    1) Read /home/user/logs/access.log, extract the 9ᵗʰ whitespace-separated
       field (that is where the HTTP status code lives in a standard combined
       Apache/Nginx log line).
    2) Count how many times each status code occurs.
    3) Sort the results according to the rules above.
    4) Write the final, *exactly formatted* lines into the file
       /home/user/output/status_code_frequency.txt.

  VERIFICATION HINTS
    • A solution that uses classic tools such as awk | sort | uniq or
      cut | sort | uniq ‑c | sort ‑nr will work; the choice is yours.
    • After you create the file, display its contents in the terminal so that
      a human reviewer can quickly see the result.
    • The automated tests will fail if:
        – the output file is missing,
        – the file path is wrong,
        – the spacing or ordering rules are violated,
        – there is an extra trailing space or no trailing newline.

  ENVIRONMENT NOTES
    • You have write permission in /home/user/output.
    • You do NOT have root access.
    • Work entirely in the terminal; do not open an editor in GUI mode.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing artefacts before the agent starts -->
  <preconditions>
    <directory path="/home/user/logs" owner="user" permissions="755"/>
    <directory path="/home/user/output" owner="user" permissions="755"/>
    <file path="/home/user/logs/access.log" permissions="644"><![CDATA[
127.0.0.1 - - [01/Jan/2023:10:00:00 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1024
192.168.0.10 - - [01/Jan/2023:10:00:01 +0000] "GET /about HTTP/1.1" 200 512
10.0.0.5 - - [01/Jan/2023:10:00:02 +0000] "POST /login HTTP/1.1" 401 256
127.0.0.1 - - [01/Jan/2023:10:00:03 +0000] "GET /doesnotexist HTTP/1.1" 404 128
127.0.0.1 - - [01/Jan/2023:10:00:04 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1024
10.0.0.5 - - [01/Jan/2023:10:00:05 +0000] "POST /login HTTP/1.1" 401 256
192.168.0.10 - - [01/Jan/2023:10:00:06 +0000] "GET /contact HTTP/1.1" 200 512
192.168.0.11 - - [01/Jan/2023:10:00:07 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 500 128
127.0.0.1 - - [01/Jan/2023:10:00:08 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1024
192.168.0.11 - - [01/Jan/2023:10:00:09 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 500 128
10.0.0.5 - - [01/Jan/2023:10:00:10 +0000] "POST /login HTTP/1.1" 401 256
192.168.0.10 - - [01/Jan/2023:10:00:11 +0000] "GET /about HTTP/1.1" 200 512
]]></file>
  </preconditions>

  <!-- What the test harness will look for after the agent finishes -->
  <postconditions>
    <file path="/home/user/output/status_code_frequency.txt" permissions="644"><![CDATA[
6 200
3 401
2 500
1 404
]]></file>
  </postconditions>
